title: The biggest limitation of AI in trading might be… defining what NOT to trade
author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
selftext:
Most discussions around AI in trading focus on:

\- predicting price  

\- generating signals  

\- optimizing entries  

But I’m starting to think the real challenge is somewhere else:

👉 defining what not to trade.

Because in real markets:

\- many setups are “almost valid”  

\- structure isn’t always clean  

\- context can be interpreted in multiple ways  

And that’s exactly where traders make mistakes.

So the question becomes:

Can AI reliably identify low-quality or unclear setups?

Not just good ones — but the ones that should be avoided.

In theory, filtering bad trades might be more valuable than finding good ones.

Curious to hear from people here:

👉 Is filtering (no trade decisions) something you’ve tried to model?  

👉 Or do most systems still focus mainly on signal generation?

comments.author: Actual_Resort1892
comments.body:
even humans most often times than not cannot really differentiate low-quality between unclear setups

I understand the point you're coming from but the general perspective shift widely between different traders systems.

With a clear logic and clear parameters the AI should follow (mathematical calculations etc), is the question about 'defining what NOT to trade' still relevant? If the rules are clear enough for the AI to follow, then no.

If the system thinks, calculate, monitor and executes fully by it own with no human feedback or logic, then extensive training on 'defining what NOT to trade' is needed.   
  
This applies to critical algorithms like Neural differential equations, neural controlled differential equations, quantum differential algorithms etc... these are researched based algorithms used extensively for AI timeseries analysis --they think and act purely based on what they were trained on, so extensive training on 'defining what NOT to trade' is needed but insanely different and a lot more easier when you left it train on what it understands.

replies.author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
replies.body:
hat’s a very fair point — and I think the distinction you’re making is key.

If you’re operating with a fully rule-based system with clearly defined parameters, then “what not to trade” is already embedded in the logic. In that case, it’s not really an AI problem, it’s just a matter of how well the rules define valid vs invalid conditions.

Where it gets more interesting is when you move into systems that rely on learned behavior rather than explicit rules.

Like you said, models based on time series learning (neural CDEs, differential approaches, etc.) don’t inherently “know” what not to trade — they only reflect what they’ve been exposed to. So if unclear or low-quality conditions aren’t properly represented or penalized during training, they’ll still try to operate in those regimes.

I think that’s where the question stays relevant:  
👉 not in defining rules, but in defining *boundaries of confidence* within learned systems.

Because even with strong models, the real challenge isn’t just acting correctly — it’s knowing when the signal itself isn’t reliable enough to act on.

Curious how you handle that part — do you explicitly model confidence thresholds, or let the system operate continuously?
